narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at past students' profiles—like grades, test scores, family background, and study plans—to predict if someone is likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and it flagged a 61.2% chance that you might not, which crossed the threshold for denial to protect the school's outcomes. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 is right around the typical student's, and your LSAT score of 35 is close to average too, while coming from an upper-middle-income family puts you in a solid spot compared to many. You're planning full-time studies, just like over 90% of applicants, which is the norm. But the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward bar failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st—much lower than the average 5th or 6th decile—suggesting your later undergrad performance placed you near the bottom of your class, a strong red flag for bar success in the model's eyes. Your Year 1 decile of 2nd, also below the typical mid-range, added to that concern by reinforcing a pattern of lower rankings early on. On a brighter note, your LSAT score helped pull the prediction toward passing, as it showed solid reasoning skills despite being a bit under average, and your GPA offered some balance too. Your family income and full-time plans had smaller influences but didn't sway things much either way. Compared to students who typically get approved, your lower grade deciles stand out as the key gap, while your other strengths align more closely with the pack. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles through targeted prep. Next, I recommend focusing on boosting your academic deciles in any remaining coursework, retaking the LSAT for a higher score, and seeking bar prep resources tailored for diverse students like yourself; consider reapplying after building that record, perhaps with a part-time option if it fits better. You've got resilience on your side—keep pushing forward.
